ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Vital Signs Assessment
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Vital Signs Assessment | Procedures & Skills | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Vital Signs Assessment for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Vital Signs Assessment** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **vital signs assessment** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: Before beginning Vital Signs Assessment, what is the nurse's best first priority on NCLEX-RN?
options: Complete unrelated charting to save time
Verify the correct patient, indications, allergies, and orders; prepare equipment and explain the procedure
Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
preTest.rationale: Safe procedure nursing begins with verification, preparation, and communication before performing the skill.

postTest.question: During a procedure, the client develops new hypotension and confusion. What should the nurse do first?
options: Finish the procedure quickly before reassessing
Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Oral Medication Administration for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Oral Medication Administration** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **oral medication administration** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: Before beginning Oral Medication Administration, what is the nurse's best first priority on NCLEX-RN?
options: Complete unrelated charting to save time
Verify the correct patient, indications, allergies, and orders; prepare equipment and explain the procedure
Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
preTest.rationale: Safe procedure nursing begins with verification, preparation, and communication before performing the skill.

postTest.question: During a procedure, the client develops new hypotension and confusion. What should the nurse do first?
options: Finish the procedure quickly before reassessing
Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

sections.body:
**IV Insertion and Care** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **iv insertion and care** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: Before beginning IV Insertion and Care, what is the nurse's best first priority on NCLEX-RN?
options: Complete unrelated charting to save time
Verify the correct patient, indications, allergies, and orders; prepare equipment and explain the procedure
Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
preTest.rationale: Safe procedure nursing begins with verification, preparation, and communication before performing the skill.

postTest.question: During a procedure, the client develops new hypotension and confusion. What should the nurse do first?
options: Finish the procedure quickly before reassessing
Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

**Tracheostomy Care** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **tracheostomy care** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: Before beginning Tracheostomy Care, what is the nurse's best first priority on NCLEX-RN?
options: Complete unrelated charting to save time
Verify the correct patient, indications, allergies, and orders; prepare equipment and explain the procedure
Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
preTest.rationale: Safe procedure nursing begins with verification, preparation, and communication before performing the skill.

postTest.question: During a procedure, the client develops new hypotension and confusion. What should the nurse do first?
options: Finish the procedure quickly before reassessing
Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

**Metered-dose inhaler (MDI) with spacer (exam technique).** Shake the canister (if drug-specific), **attach spacer**, seal lips around mouthpiece (or mask fit for children), **actuate one puff** into the spacer, then **inhale slowly and deeply** (or **tidal breathing** in small children per teaching protocol), **hold breath ~10 seconds** if able, and **wait** between puffs per orders—typically **30–60 seconds** between actuations for bronchodilators. Rinse mouth after **corticosteroid** MDIs to reduce **thrush** risk. Teach patients to **prime** new devices per manufacturer insert, **clean spacer** weekly with water (not alcohol—static cling reduces dose delivery), and **count actuations** when the device lacks a dose counter.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Urinary Catheter Insertion for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Urinary Catheter Insertion** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **urinary catheter insertion** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:

ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Drain Care (JP and Hemovac) | Procedures & Skills | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Drain Care (JP and Hemovac) for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Drain Care (JP and Hemovac)** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **drain care (jp and hemovac)** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: Before beginning Drain Care (JP and Hemovac), what is the nurse's best first priority on NCLEX-RN?
options: Complete unrelated charting to save time
Verify the correct patient, indications, allergies, and orders; prepare equipment and explain the procedure
Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
preTest.rationale: Safe procedure nursing begins with verification, preparation, and communication before performing the skill.

postTest.question: During a procedure, the client develops new hypotension and confusion. What should the nurse do first?
options: Finish the procedure quickly before reassessing
Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: NGN Procedures Case Studies
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: NGN Procedures Case Studies | Procedures & Skills | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: NGN Procedures Case Studies for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**NGN Procedures Case Studies** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **ngn procedures case studies** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: PPE Use and Removal
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: PPE Use and Removal | Procedures & Skills | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: PPE Use and Removal for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**PPE Use and Removal** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **ppe use and removal** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

options: Complete unrelated charting to save time

Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
preTest.rationale: Safe procedure nursing begins with verification, preparation, and communication before performing the skill.

us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: High-Alert Medication Handling | Procedures & Skills | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: High-Alert Medication Handling for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**High-Alert Medication Handling** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **high-alert medication handling** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Suctioning (Oral and Nasopharyngeal) for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Suctioning (Oral and Nasopharyngeal)** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **suctioning (oral and nasopharyngeal)** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

preTest.question: Before beginning Suctioning (Oral and Nasopharyngeal), what is the nurse's best first priority on NCLEX-RN?
options: Complete unrelated charting to save time
Verify the correct patient, indications, allergies, and orders; prepare equipment and explain the procedure
Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
preTest.rationale: Safe procedure nursing begins with verification, preparation, and communication before performing the skill.

postTest.question: During a procedure, the client develops new hypotension and confusion. What should the nurse do first?
options: Finish the procedure quickly before reassessing
Stop or pause the procedure as appropriate, remain with the patient, reassess vitals, and notify the provider with objective data
Send the family out without explanation
Increase the procedure rate to shorten exposure time
postTest.rationale: New hemodynamic or neuro changes require immediate safety actions: pause the task, reassess, and escalate per protocol.

- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

**Metered-dose inhaler (MDI) with spacer (exam technique).** Shake the canister (if drug-specific), **attach spacer**, seal lips around mouthpiece (or mask fit for children), **actuate one puff** into the spacer, then **inhale slowly and deeply** (or **tidal breathing** in small children per teaching protocol), **hold breath ~10 seconds** if able, and **wait** between puffs per orders—typically **30–60 seconds** between actuations for bronchodilators. Rinse mouth after **corticosteroid** MDIs to reduce **thrush** risk. Teach patients to **prime** new devices per manufacturer insert, **clean spacer** weekly with water (not alcohol—static cling reduces dose delivery), and **count actuations** when the device lacks a dose counter.

us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Specimen Collection (Urine, Stool, Sputum) for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Specimen Collection (Urine, Stool, Sputum)** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **specimen collection (urine, stool, sputum)** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:

- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: What Do You Do First? (Procedures & Skills)
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: What Do You Do First? (Procedures & Skills) | Procedures & Skills | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: What Do You Do First? (Procedures & Skills) for NCLEX-RN: nursing priorities, safety checks, patient teaching, and clinical judgment cues aligned with US and Canadian RN practice.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**What Do You Do First? (Procedures & Skills)** is a core **Procedures & Skills** competency for NCLEX-RN: you must match **assessment** to **indication**, follow **policy and orders**, protect **airway–breathing–circulation** when instability appears, and document **objective** findings with **timestamps**. Boards reward **sequence** (verify identity, indications, allergies, equipment integrity) before **speed**.

Connect this skill to **infection prevention** ([PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics)), **deterioration recognition** ([sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy)), and your pathway hubs: [Canada RN lessons](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN lessons](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives:** state indications and contraindications for this procedure in acute care; list required supplies and monitoring; identify when to **stop**, **reassess**, and **escalate** to the provider or rapid response.

**Nursing priorities:** verify **consent/orders** when applicable; maintain **asepsis** or **clean technique** per stem; protect **privacy** and **dignity**; teach using **teach-back** after stabilization.

sections.heading: Why NCLEX-RN tests this
sections.body:
Examiners embed **Procedures & Skills** items as **first action**, **priority**, or **NGN case** questions. Expect traps that **skip verification**, **expand scope**, **delegate unstable assessment** to UAP, or **continue** a procedure when **new red flags** appear (hypotension, hypoxia, new confusion, uncontrolled bleeding, severe pain).

Eliminate answers that **delay reporting** critical changes, **violate isolation** or **PPE** sequence, or **rush teaching** during active instability. When two tasks compete, choose the option that **reduces imminent harm** first.

sections.heading: Core concepts and safety
sections.body:
- **Preparation:** gather equipment, perform hand hygiene, explain steps, position the patient, ensure **privacy**.
- **Execution:** follow **rights** of medication administration when applicable; maintain **sterile field** integrity when the stem specifies sterile technique; label specimens at **bedside** per policy.
- **Monitoring:** repeat **vitals** and focused assessments per frequency; inspect **sites** (IV, wound, catheter) for infection and device integrity.
- **Red flags / safety:** sudden **dyspnea**, **chest pain**, **hypotension**, **neuro change**, **bleeding**, **anaphylaxis** signs—**stop** the procedure as appropriate, **stay with the patient**, **call for help**, and **notify the provider** with objective data.
- **Patient teaching:** use clear language; confirm **understanding**; include **when to call** the nurse and **activity** or **diet** restrictions tied to the procedure.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A 68-year-old client is assigned for **what do you do first? (procedures & skills)** during your shift. Mid-procedure they report **new** **shortness of breath** and **dizziness**. Vitals show **hypotension** compared with baseline and **tachycardia**.

**Fork:** **Stop** the procedure if continuing would risk harm; **stay** with the patient; **raise the bed** if ordered; **apply oxygen** per protocol; **repeat vitals**; **notify the provider** using **SBAR** with trends—not completion of paperwork first. This tests whether you **prioritize assessment and escalation** over task completion.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- **Verify → perform → monitor → document** is the backbone of safe procedures.
- **New instability** during a skill **pauses** the task and **triggers** reassessment and escalation.
- **Synthesis:** NCLEX rewards **patient-centered** sequencing and **scope-safe** nursing actions.

**Related:** [Sepsis early recognition](LESSON:sepsis-early-recognition-hy) · [PPE & transmission basics](LESSON:ppe-transmission-basics) · [Canada RN hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons) · [US RN hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

options: Complete unrelated charting to save time
Verify the correct patient, indications, allergies, and orders; prepare equipment and explain the procedure
Delegate all monitoring to the UAP while the nurse leaves the unit
Assume consent without checking policy
